quotes.verbatim_quote:
we can play 1/8th note
jazz lines that begin on a scale note and cause all the strong chord tones to
fall on the beat, and the chromatic passing notes on the off-beat. This is
useful, because in hardbop / bebop jazz, chromatic notes are generally placed
on the off-beats.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Foundational rule of the chapter: chromatic passing notes belong on off-beats.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
I tend to play these exercises using a legato technique with lots of hammer-
ons, pull-offs and slides. Aim to pick a chromatic note and resolve it to the
following note with a legato movement. This should result in nice phrasing,
because the passing note is accented and the scale note is softer. To aid this
approach, I try to keep passing notes and scale notes on the same string where
possible.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Primary technique instruction for executing chromatic lines, with both mechanics and phrasing rationale.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
A chromatic enclosure is a short phrase that mixes scale notes and chromatic
passing notes to emphasise a target note. Think of it in these terms: you want
to highlight one particular note, but surround it with other notes that lead to
it. In jazz, enclosures can range in length anything from two to ten notes.

The concept is, for each note
of a C major triad, to play a chromatic note below and a diatonic note above.
Example 2e shows how this sounds for a C major triad in the 8th position.

The first two bars of this lick place the chromatic note first, followed by the
diatonic note, then the triad note. The second two bars flip this sequence
around — the diatonic note is played first, then the chromatic note, then the
triad note. Either way sounds good, so you can find your preference.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the exact construction rule for two-note enclosures with both orderings as valid options.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
We can take this idea a step further with four-note enclosures. These are very
common in modern jazz, since they provide an easy way to emphasise the
basic jazz groove, in which the “heavy” beats fall on the 1 and the 3. With
more notes to play with, there can be more permutations of the sequence.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Explains why four-note enclosures are used — to align resolution with strong beats 1 and 3.

quotes.topic: arpeggio from 5th degree over II chord
quotes.verbatim_quote:
Playing the arpeggio from the 5th degree of the minor seventh chord creates a
nice sound over a II chord, but you could consider adding the 3rd of the Dm7
chord, since that note is not in the arpeggio. Adding the third helps to clearly
define the sound of the underlying chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Reusable harmonic rule for generating characteristic bebop lines over II chords.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Playing an arpeggio from the 3rd of a chord is a useful device to produce
characteristic bebop lines.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names and endorses a core bebop substitution principle.

quotes.topic: diatonic note substitution for chromatic gap
quotes.verbatim_quote:
Since it’s
not possible to add a chromatic note in between, I added a G note (the
diatonic note above F). Listen to how natural that approach sounds.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Practical rule for handling cases where a half-step gap doesn't exist.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Four-note enclosures can work in two ways. They can precede a chord tone
to create a suspended sound, or they can be used as a way of “pulling”
towards the chord tone you intend to resolve to.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Conceptual taxonomy of four-note enclosure usage: suspension vs. tension-and-pull.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The chromatic enclosure in this bar is
placed on beat 2, so that it will resolve on beat 4 of the bar. This leaves a
chromatic note on beat 3, which is a strong beat in jazz. The effect is to give
the line an unresolved quality, again reminiscent of Metheny. Using this idea
throughout a solo can open things up, because the melodic lines will sound
less connected to the underlying chords, achieving a more modern sound.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Advanced rhythmic displacement rule — placing an enclosure on beat 2 to resolve on beat 4.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Chromatic enclosures and passing notes are a big part of the sound we
associate with the bebop and hardbop melodic language. However, be careful
to exercise some control over how you use them: remain aware that their
main purpose is to target or gravitate towards scale / chord tones.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Closing rule warning against overuse and anchoring the technique to resolution.
